subject: History
q: Who established the Tukulor empire?
explanation: Tukulor empire, Tukulor also spelled Tukolor, or Toucouleur, Muslim theocracy that flourished in the 19th century in western Africa from Senegal eastward to Timbuktu (Tombouctou). The founder of the empire is Al-Ḥajj ʿUmar.

subject: History
q: Emperor Menelik II defeated Italian troops in the battle of
explanation: The Battle of Adwa was the climactic battle of the First Italo-Ethiopian War. The Ethiopian forces defeated the Italian invading force.

subject: History
q: The Nigerian second republic began in the year ____
explanation: The Second Nigerian Republic was a brief formation of the Nigerian state which succeeded the military governments formed after the overthrow of the first republic. It lasted from 1979-1983.

subject: History
q: The __________ were the first missionaries to arrive Nigeria.
explanation: The Catholics, through the influence of the Portuguese traders, were the first missionaries to set foot on Nigerian soil. The primary objective of the early Christian missionaries was to convert the 'heathen' or the benighted African to Christianity via education.

subject: History
q: ________ preceded Nelson Mandela as president of South Africa.
explanation: De Klerk was a South African politician who served as state president of South Africa from 1989 to 1994 and as deputy president from 1994 to 1996 in the democratic government. As South Africa's last head of state from the era of white-minority rule, he and his government dismantled the apartheid system and introduced universal suffrage. Ideologically a conservative and an economic liberal, he led the National Party (NP) from 1989 to 1997.

subject: History
q: What does the name Liberia mean?
explanation: Settled in the early 1800s by freeborn Blacks and former slaves from America, Liberia, whose name means "land of freedom," has always struggled with its double cultural heritage: that of the settlers and of the indigenous Africans.

subject: History
q: The first coup in Nigeria was led by
explanation: The 1966 Nigerian coup d'état began on 15 January 1966, when mutinous Nigerian soldiers led by Chukwuma Kaduna Nzeogwu and Emmanuel Ifeajuna killed 22 people including the Prime Minister of Nigeria, many senior politicians, many senior Army officers (including their wives), and sentinels on protective duty.

subject: History
q: In what year was ECOWAS established?
explanation: The Economic Community Of West African States (ECOWAS) is a regional group of fifteen countries, founded in 1975.

subject: History
q: NYM(Nigerian Youth Movement) evolved from _____.
explanation: The NYM was formed by Lagos nationalist led by Ernest Ikoli, H.O Davies, Dr. J.C Vaughan and Samuel Akinsanya in 1934. The party was first called Lagos Youth Movement by 1936 other Nigerian from other parts then changed to Nigerian Youth Movement.

subject: History
q: The __________ constitution introduced federalism in Nigeria.
explanation: Bernard Bourdillon the Governor-general at that time initiated and laid the foundation of federalism in Nigeria in 1939 by creating three provinces. He later handed over the constitution to his successor Arthur Richards and it became the Richards Constitution of 1946.

subject: History
q: ________ moved the motion for Nigeria's independence.
explanation: In 1953, Enahoro became the first to move the motion for Nigeria's independence which was eventually granted in 1960 after several political setbacks and defeats in parliament. Enahoro has been regarded by academics and many Nigerians as the "Father of Nigeria State".

subject: History
q: The Northern region of Nigeria gained self-government in ____.
explanation: Western and Eastern Nigeria were both awarded self-government in 1957, while the Northern region would follow in 1959.

subject: History
q: In 1840, Sayyid Said transferred his capital from Muscat to?
explanation: In 1840 Said moved his entire household to Zanzibar and declared it the new capital of his empire. Said and his many relatives and associates built numerous palaces, bath houses and country manors on Zanzibar, and introduced the commercial farming of cloves, sugar and other crops.

subject: History
q: Nigeria practised the Westminster system of government between
explanation: The 'Westminster Model' existed in Nigeria from the time of independence in particular until the first military coup in 1966

subject: History
q: The equivalent of the Aare- ona kankanfo of the Yoruba pre-colonial system in the Hausa/Fulani pre-colonial system is the
explanation: The Aare- ona kankanfo is the head of the army in the Yoruba pre-colonial system; the equivalent is the Madawaki who is the head of the army in the Hausa/Fulani pre-colonial system. The Waziri is the prime minister; the Galadima is head of the capital while the Dogari is the head of the police.

subject: History
q: The first British consul in Nigeria was
explanation: In 1849, John Beecroft was appointed Consul of the Bights of Benin and Biafra by the British, a position he held (along with his governorship of Fernando Po) until his death in 1854

subject: History
q: The first almagamation in Nigeria happened in the year ___
explanation: In 1906, the Southern protectorate was almagamated with the Lagos colony to form the colony and protectorate of Southern Nigeria.

subject: History
q: Who was appointed by the federal government of Nigeria as the administrator of Western Region in 1962?
explanation: He was appointed sole administrator of Western Region in June 1962 after a political crisis in the region, holding office in place of the Premier Samuel Akintola until December that year.

subject: History
q: The Coussey Commission Report laid the ground-work for the eventual independence of
explanation: The recommendation of the Coussey Commission formed the basis of the 1950 constitution. It marked the beginning of the process of independence for the Gold Coast as Ghana, The first Sub -Saharan British Colony to achieve independence.

subject: History
q: Kastina and Akwa-Ibom were created in September, _____.
explanation: In September 1987 Babangida created Akwa Ibom State from part of Cross River State and Katsina State from part of Kaduna State.

subject: History
q: Nigeria joined the Organisation of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) in__________
explanation: It was on July 12, 1971, that Nigeria became the 11th Member of OPEC.

subject: History
q: Nnamdi Azikiwe's West African Pilot was established in what year?
explanation: The West African Pilot was a newspaper launched in Nigeria by Nnamdi Azikiwe in 1937, dedicated to fighting for independence from British colonial rule.

subject: History
q: When did Lagos become a colony?
explanation: Lagos was bombarded by the British in 1851, annexed on 6 August 1861 and declared a colony on 5 March 1862.

subject: History
q: The sokoto jihad began in what year
explanation: The Fulani War of 1804–1808, also known as the Fulani Jihad or Jihad of Usman dan Fodio, was a military conflict in present-day Nigeria and Cameroon.

subject: History
q: The Kanem–Bornu Empire existed in areas which are now part of the following countries except
explanation: The Kanem–Bornu Empire existed in areas which are now part of Niger, Cameroon, Central African Republic, Chad and Nigeria.

subject: History
q: The republic of Benin was formerly known as
explanation: The History of Benin since the 16th century, for the geographical area included in 1960 in what was then called the Republic of Dahomey before becoming the People's Republic of Benin.

subject: History
q: Ismail Pasha was removed as the Khedive of Egypt at the behest of Great Britain in____________
explanation: Isma'il Pasha was the Khedive of Egypt and conqueror of Sudan from 1863 to 1879, when he was removed at the behest of Great Britain.

subject: History
q: The United Nations was created as a result of the failure of the ________
explanation:
The League of Nations was the first intergovernmental organization that was established after World War 1 in order to try and maintain peace. It was headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland and designed to be a forum for handling international disputes before they flared up into military action and caused domino effects that pulled ally nations into the conflict (as had happened with the Great War). Unfortunately, the League failed miserably in its intended goal: to prevent another world war from happening (WW2 broke out only two decades later).

The idea was for the League of Nations to prevent wars through disarmament, collective security, and negotiation. It was also involved in other issues such as drug trafficking, arms trade, and global health. Although the League disbanded during WW2, it was replaced with the United Nations, which is still going strong today.

subject: History
q: During the Great Trek, the Voortrekkers clashed with the indigenous Zulu people. Who was the Zulu king at that time?
explanation: During the Great Trek, the Voortrekkers clashed with the indigenous Zulu people. The Zulu king at that time was Dingane kaSenzangakhona.

subject: History
q: Which European explorer is credited with discovering Sierra Leone in the 15th century?
explanation: Vasco da Gama

subject: History
q: Which military leader introduced the "War Against Indiscipline" campaign in the 1980s?
explanation: The 'War Against Indiscipline' campaign was introduced by General Muhammadu Buhari in March 1984. The campaign was aimed at promoting discipline, orderliness, and respect for public properties among Nigerians.

subject: History
q: The Richards Constitution of 1946 introduced a new political structure in Nigeria. What was a major criticism of this constitution by Nigerian nationalists?
options: It failed to recognize the diversity of ethnic groups in Nigeria.
It favored the interests of urban elites over rural populations.
It restricted the activities of nationalist organizations.
It granted too much power to regional governments.
explanation: The Richards Constitution of 1946 was criticized by Nigerian nationalists because it failed to recognize the diversity of ethnic groups in Nigeria. The constitution was seen as a tool for the British to continue indirect rule and did not adequately represent the interests of the various ethnic groups in the country.

subject: History
q: The Lyttleton Constitution of 1954 introduced which significant political reform in Nigeria?
options: Creation of the Office of the Governor-General
explanation: The Lyttleton Constitution of 1954 introduced the federal system in Nigeria. This was a significant political reform as it decentralized power and allowed for regional governments to have more control over their affairs.

subject: History
q: The city of Ife, in present-day Nigeria, is famous for its artistic heritage, particularly its
explanation: The city of Ife, in present-day Nigeria, is famous for its artistic heritage, particularly its Terracotta sculptures. These sculptures, often of human heads, are renowned for their naturalism and detail, and are considered among the highest achievements of African art.

subject: History
q: Who is often considered the founder of modern Egypt and the ruler of Egypt from 1805 to 1848?
explanation: Mohammed Ali is often considered the founder of modern Egypt and was the ruler of Egypt from 1805 to 1848. He is known for his military and administrative reforms that laid the groundwork for the modernization of Egypt.

subject: History
q: Which influential leader in the Sokoto Caliphate was responsible for administration and justice?
explanation: In the Sokoto Caliphate, the Waziri was responsible for administration and justice. The Waziri was the vizier or minister of the Caliphate, and his duties included overseeing the administration of the state and ensuring justice was served.

subject: History
q: Which colonial policy was implemented to extract resources and labor from Nigeria for Britain's benefit?
explanation: The colonial policy implemented by Britain in Nigeria was Indirect Rule. This system was introduced by Lord Lugard, the first Governor-General of Nigeria. Under this system, traditional rulers were allowed to govern their people as long as they followed British guidelines. This policy was primarily used to extract resources and labor from Nigeria for Britain's benefit.

subject: History
q: Which of the following was a significant trading commodity in the Nigeria area before 1800?
explanation: Before 1800, Kola nuts were a significant trading commodity in the Nigeria area. They were used in social and religious ceremonies and also as a form of currency.

subject: History
q: The Omani Empire declined in the 19th century due to
explanation: The decline of the Omani Empire in the 19th century was largely due to internal power struggles. The empire was weakened by a series of succession disputes and civil wars.

subject: History
q: Morocco is known for producing which luxury commodity, derived from the argan tree?
explanation: Morocco is known for producing a luxury commodity derived from the argan tree, which is Argan oil. Argan oil is used in high-end cosmetic products and gourmet cooking, and is unique to the region of Morocco.

subject: History
q: Which pre-colonial kingdom in the Nigeria Area was renowned for its wealth and participation in the trans-Saharan trade?
explanation: The Kanem-Bornu Empire, located in the Nigeria Area, was renowned for its wealth and participation in the trans-Saharan trade. The empire was a center of Islamic learning and trade, with connections stretching from North Africa to the Arabian Peninsula.

subject: History
q: Which major event led to the decline of the Benin Kingdom's influence in the 19th century?
explanation: The decline of the Benin Kingdom's influence in the 19th century was majorly due to the Scramble for Africa. The Scramble for Africa was a period of rapid colonization of African territories by European powers in the late 19th century, which often led to the decline or destruction of existing African kingdoms and empires.

subject: History
q: The British colonial policy of "indirect rule" was first implemented in Nigeria by Lugard in which region?
explanation: The British colonial policy of 'indirect rule' was first implemented in Nigeria by Lord Lugard in the Northern region, specifically in Kano. Indirect rule is a system of governance used by the British to control parts of their colonial empire, particularly in Africa and Asia, through pre-existing local power structures. These dependencies were often called 'protectorates' or 'trucial states'.

subject: History
q: The Aba Women's Riots of 1929 were a response to:
options: The prohibition of women from participating in politics.
The banning of traditional religious practices.
The imposition of heavy taxes on women traders.
explanation: The Aba Women's Riots of 1929, also known as the Women's War, was a response to the imposition of heavy taxes on women traders by the British colonial administration. The women protested against the Warrant Chiefs, who were representatives of the colonial government, and their oppressive taxation policies.

subject: History
q: The Kanem-Borno Empire prospered due to its control over
explanation: The Kanem-Borno Empire prospered due to its control over the salt trade. The empire was strategically located on trans-Saharan trade routes, and it capitalized on this by controlling the trade of salt, a valuable commodity at the time.

subject: History
q: Apartheid in South Africa was a system that institutionalized racial segregation and discrimination. What year did it officially begin?
explanation: Apartheid officially began in South Africa in 1948. It was a system of institutionalized racial segregation and discrimination that lasted until the early 1990s. The National Party, which came to power in 1948, formalized and expanded segregationist laws into the apartheid system.

subject: History
q: The colonization of Liberia began in what year?
explanation: The colonization of Liberia began in the year 1822. Liberia was founded by the American Colonization Society (ACS) as a place to send free African-Americans, who were freed from slavery but faced racial discrimination in the United States. The ACS believed black people would face better chances for full lives in Africa.

subject: History
q: What was the outcome of the Battle of Adwa in 1896?
options: Ethiopia and Italy signed a peace treaty.
Ethiopia gained full independence from Italy.
explanation: The outcome of the Battle of Adwa in 1896 was that Ethiopia gained full independence from Italy. The battle was a decisive victory for Ethiopia and marked the end of Italy's imperial ambitions in Africa.

subject: History
q: The ancient city of Carthage, once a powerful Phoenician civilization, was located in modern-day
explanation: The ancient city of Carthage, once a powerful Phoenician civilization, was located in modern-day Tunisia. Carthage was a significant power in the Mediterranean and fought against Rome in the Punic Wars.

subject: History
q: Which organization played a crucial role in the fight against apartheid in South Africa and was led by Nelson Mandela?
options: Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS)
explanation: The African National Congress (ANC) played a crucial role in the fight against apartheid in South Africa. Nelson Mandela, who later became the first black president of South Africa, was a prominent leader of the ANC.

subject: History
q: Who was the Oba of Benin during the height of the kingdom's power in the 19th century?
explanation: Oba Ovonramwen was the Oba of Benin during the height of the kingdom's power in the 19th century. He was the ruler when the Punitive Expedition of 1897 occurred, in which the British exiled him and burned and looted the royal palace, marking the end of the Kingdom of Benin.

subject: History
q: The decline of the Songhai Empire in the 16th century was partly due to
explanation: The decline of the Songhai Empire in the 16th century was due to a combination of internal unrest and external attacks, particularly from the Moroccan army. The other options are not historically accurate.

subject: History
q: What was the major consequence of the Scramble for Africa in Yorubaland?
options: Yorubaland lost its independence and became a British protectorate.
explanation: The major consequence of the Scramble for Africa in Yorubaland was that Yorubaland lost its independence and became a British protectorate. The Scramble for Africa was a period of rapid colonization of African territories by European powers in the late 19th century. In the case of Yorubaland, it was the British who took control, thereby making it a British protectorate.

subject: History
q: Which conference, held in 1884-1885, laid the groundwork for the partition of Africa among European powers?
explanation: The Conference of Berlin, also known as the Berlin Conference, was held from 1884 to 1885. It was organized by Otto von Bismarck, the first chancellor of Germany, and involved representatives from various European nations. The main aim of the conference was to divide Africa among the European powers, thereby laying the groundwork for the partition of Africa. This is a significant event in history as it led to the 'Scramble for Africa', where European powers rushed to claim African territories.

subject: History
q: Which African country experienced a prolonged and violent struggle for independence against French colonial rule, culminating in a war of independence that lasted from 1954 to 1962?
explanation: Algeria experienced a prolonged and violent struggle for independence against French colonial rule, culminating in a war of independence that lasted from 1954 to 1962. The war ended with Algeria gaining its independence from France. This war is known for its guerrilla warfare and the use of torture by both sides, and it is considered one of the most important decolonization wars.

subject: History
q: Which Yoruba city-state was the last to fall to British colonial rule in the late 19th century?
explanation: Abeokuta was the last Yoruba city-state to fall to British colonial rule in the late 19th century. The British had already established control over other Yoruba city-states like Lagos, Oyo, and Ibadan. However, Abeokuta resisted British rule for a long time before finally succumbing to it.

subject: History
q: How did the establishment of missionary schools during the colonial era affect the Nigerian economy?
options: It led to increased government spending on education.
It hindered economic development due to religious conflicts.
It improved the literacy rate and provided a skilled workforce.
It favored British education over traditional Nigerian education.
explanation: The establishment of missionary schools during the colonial era had a positive impact on the Nigerian economy. These schools improved the literacy rate and provided a skilled workforce. The education provided by these schools enabled Nigerians to take up administrative and professional roles, thereby contributing to economic development.

subject: History
q: Lake Victoria, one of the largest freshwater lakes in the world, is shared by three countries. Which of the following countries is NOT among them?
explanation: Lake Victoria is one of the largest freshwater lakes in the world and is shared by three countries: Uganda, Kenya, and Tanzania. Mozambique is not among the countries that share Lake Victoria.

subject: History
q: Which British explorer is credited with the "discovery" of the Niger River, sparking further interest in the Nigeria Area?
explanation: The British explorer credited with the 'discovery' of the Niger River is Mungo Park, but since his name is not in the options, none of the options is correct. The Niger River was already known to the local inhabitants, but Park's exploration brought it to the attention of the European world.

subject: History
q: Which Islamic reform movement aimed to purify the practice of Islam from local customs and traditions in West Africa during the 18th and 19th centuries?
explanation: The Mahdist Movement was an Islamic reform movement that aimed to purify the practice of Islam from local customs and traditions in West Africa during the 18th and 19th centuries. The movement was led by Muhammad Ahmad bin Abd Allah, who declared himself the Mahdi, the prophesied redeemer of Islam who will appear at end times to rid the world of evil and injustice.

subject: History
q: In 1991, Nigeria hosted the "World Festival of Black Arts and Culture." Which city was the venue for this cultural event?
explanation: The World Festival of Black Arts and Culture, also known as FESTAC, was hosted by Nigeria in 1991. The event took place in Lagos, which is Nigeria's most populous city and a major cultural hub.

subject: History
q: During the Mahdiyya Movement, the British-Egyptian forces were defeated at the Battle of
explanation: The Battle of Khartoum was a significant battle during the Mahdiyya Movement where the British-Egyptian forces were defeated. The Mahdiyya Movement was a revolutionary Sudanese Islamic order in the late 19th century that rose against the oppressive Egyptian and British colonial rule.

subject: History
q: During the Second Republic, Nigeria experienced a boom in which sector?
explanation: During the Second Republic, Nigeria experienced a boom in the oil and gas sector. The 1970s and early 1980s were a period of significant oil wealth for Nigeria, due to the increase in oil prices.

subject: History
q: What significant event occurred during the Murtala/Obasanjo regime's early days in office?
options: The declaration of war against neighboring countries
The launch of the Green Revolution program
explanation: During the early days of the Murtala/Obasanjo regime, one of the significant events was the release of political prisoners. This was part of the regime's efforts to promote national reconciliation and unity.

subject: History
q: Who was the Zulu king responsible for the expansion of the Zulu Kingdom during the MFECANE?
explanation: Shaka Zulu was the king of the Zulu Kingdom from 1816 to 1828. He is credited with creating a fighting force that devastated the entire region, in a period known as the Mfecane or 'crushing'. His reforms to the army and innovative tactics made the Zulu one of the most powerful forces in South Africa.

subject: History
q: Which social group in Nigeria was most affected by the introduction of the indirect rule system?
explanation: The indirect rule system, introduced by the British, greatly affected the traditional rulers in Nigeria. This system used the existing traditional rulers and institutions to govern, thereby increasing their power and influence.

subject: History
q: Which constitutional conference in London paved the way for Nigeria's independence?
explanation: The London Conference of 1957 was a significant event that paved the way for Nigeria's independence. It was at this conference that the British government agreed to grant Nigeria self-governance in all matters except defense and foreign affairs. This decision set the stage for full independence, which was achieved in 1960.

subject: History
q: Which African leader famously stated, "I am the captain of my soul" and became an icon of the decolonization movement?
explanation: Nelson Mandela famously stated, "I am the captain of my soul." This quote is from the poem 'Invictus' by William Ernest Henley, which Mandela recited to other prisoners while he was incarcerated. Mandela became an icon of the decolonization movement in Africa, particularly in his home country of South Africa.

subject: History
q: The Mfecane was primarily driven by
explanation: The Mfecane, which translates to 'The Crushing' in Zulu, was a period of widespread chaos and warfare among indigenous ethnic communities in southern Africa during the period between 1815 and about 1840. It was primarily driven by intertribal conflicts and population movements.

subject: History
q: What was the political structure of the Nupe Kingdom during this period?
explanation: The Nupe Kingdom was traditionally ruled by an Etsu, or king, making it an absolute monarchy. In this system, the king has complete power and authority over the state and its people.

subject: History
q: What was the primary economic activity of the Nupe people during this period?
explanation: In this century, farming was predominant.

subject: History
q: How did the amalgamation affect the traditional political systems in Nigeria?
options: It had no impact on the traditional systems
It led to the formation of a new political system
explanation: The amalgamation of Nigeria in 1914 by Lord Lugard led to the formation of a new political system. It brought together the Northern and Southern Protectorates and the Colony of Lagos under a single administration, creating a political entity and system that was different from the traditional systems.

subject: History
q: Which of the following was a significant effect of colonial education in Nigeria?
options: Fostering unity among various ethnic groups
Limited access to education for the masses
Preservation of indigenous languages and cultures
explanation: One significant effect of colonial education in Nigeria was the limited access to education for the masses. The colonial education system was designed to serve the interests of the colonial powers and was not widely accessible to the general population. It was mainly focused on providing basic literacy skills and training in trades and professions that served the colonial administration.

subject: History
q: Which external influence contributed to the spread of the Bantu language in the Nigeria area?
explanation: The interaction with Arab merchants was an external factor that influenced.

subject: History
q: Which Christian missionary is known for translating the Bible into several indigenous Liberian languages?
explanation: Samuel Ajayi Crowther is known for translating the Bible into several indigenous Liberian languages. He was a linguist and the first African Anglican bishop in Nigeria. His work in translation was significant in the spread of Christianity in West Africa.

subject: History
q: Which major international power supported Biafra during the Civil War?
explanation: During the Biafra Civil War, France was the major international power that supported Biafra.

subject: History
q: How did Ethiopia's defeat of Italian forces at the Battle of Adwa impact the Pan-African movement?
options: It inspired other African nations to join the movement
It encouraged African diaspora communities to return to Africa
It led to the formation of the African Union
It accelerated the end of colonialism in Africa
explanation: Ethiopia's defeat of Italian forces at the Battle of Adwa in 1896 had a significant impact on the Pan-African movement. It inspired other African nations to resist colonial rule and join the movement for independence and self-determination.

subject: History
q: The "Magna Carta," signed in 1215, limited the power of which monarch?
options: Emperor Charlemagne of the Holy Roman Empire
explanation: The Magna Carta was signed by King John of England in 1215. It was a charter of liberties that required the King to proclaim certain liberties, and accept that his will was not arbitrary, effectively limiting his powers.

subject: History
q: Sierra Leone is home to a rich musical tradition, with one popular genre known as
explanation: The popular genre of music in Sierra Leone is known as 'Palm-wine' music or 'Maringa'.

subject: History
q: The Mahdiyya Movement began with the declaration of the Mahdi in what year?
explanation: The Mahdiyya Movement began with the declaration of the Mahdi in the year 1885. The Mahdiyya Movement was an Islamic revolt against the Egyptian military occupation of Sudan. It was led by Muhammad Ahmad bin Abd Allah, who declared himself the Mahdi, the prophesied redeemer of the Islamic faith, in 1881, but it wasn't until 1885 that the movement gained significant traction.

subject: History
q: How did the Murtala/Obasanjo regime handle the issue of minority rights and tensions among various ethnic groups?
options: Encouraging forced assimilation of minority cultures
Implementing a quota system in government employment and education
Imposing martial law in regions with conflicts
Granting autonomy to regions seeking independence
explanation: The Murtala/Obasanjo regime in Nigeria implemented a quota system in government employment and education to handle the issue of minority rights and tensions among various ethnic groups. This was done to ensure fair representation and equal opportunities for all ethnic groups in the country.

subject: History
q: Which region of Nigeria was known for its active involvement in long-distance trade during the pre-1800 period?
explanation: The Niger Delta region of Nigeria was known for its active involvement in long-distance trade during the pre-1800 period. The region's location along the Niger River made it a hub for trade and commerce.

subject: History
q: The movement led by El Hadj Umar Tall in the 19th century aimed to create an Islamic state in
explanation: El Hadj Umar Tall was a West African political leader, Islamic scholar, and Tijani Sufi who founded a brief empire encompassing much of what is now Guinea, Senegal, and Mali in the 19th century. His movement aimed to create an Islamic state in Mali.

subject: History
q: Which international boycott significantly contributed to the end of apartheid in South Africa?
explanation: The Anti-Apartheid Boycott significantly contributed to the end of apartheid in South Africa. This international movement was initiated in response to the policy of apartheid and involved numerous boycotts and sanctions against South Africa. These actions were instrumental in putting pressure on the South African government to end apartheid.

subject: History
q: In which year did Nigeria host the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting (CHOGM)?
explanation: Nigeria hosted the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting (CHOGM) in the year 2003. CHOGM is a biennial summit meeting of the heads of government from all Commonwealth nations. Every two years the meeting is held in a different member state, and is chaired by that nation's respective Prime Minister or President who becomes the Commonwealth Chairperson-in-Office.

subject: History
q: The Omani influence in East Africa led to the growth of a distinct Swahili culture. What contributed to the development of this culture?
explanation: The Omani influence in East Africa led to the growth of a distinct Swahili culture. This was largely due to Arab and Persian influences.

subject: History
q: What was the primary reason for the military takeover by the Abacha regime?
explanation: The primary reason for the military takeover by the Abacha regime was to restore democracy. This is a common reason given by military regimes when they seize power, often citing corruption, inefficiency, or instability in the existing democratic government. However, it's important to note that the actual outcomes of such takeovers often do not align with these stated goals.

subject: History
q: Which historical period is characterized by the emergence of written language and the invention of the wheel?
explanation: The Bronze Age is characterized by the emergence of written language and the invention of the wheel. This period marked the first time humans started to work with metal. Bronze tools and weapons, brick and stone buildings, and irrigation systems were new innovations of this period. The invention of the wheel is also attributed to this period.

subject: History
q: What was the purpose of the Pan-African Congresses that began in 1900?
options: To advocate for African colonization of Europe
To promote European imperialism in Africa
To discuss strategies for African independence and unity
To establish trade alliances with Asian countries
explanation: The Pan-African Congresses were a series of meetings held in the 20th century that aimed to address the issues facing Africa as a result of European colonization. The main purpose of these congresses was to discuss strategies for African independence and unity.

subject: History
q: The system of Government in the Sokoto Caliphate was
explanation: The system of Government in the Sokoto Caliphate was a form of Totalitarianism as the Emir was supreme.

subject: History
q: The first colonial capital of Nigeria was established in
explanation: The first colonial capital of Nigeria was established in Calabar. Calabar served as the first capital of Nigeria in the early stages of Nigeria's colonial period, before the capital was moved to Lagos.

subject: History
q: The Great Walls of Kano, ancient defensive walls, were built to protect the city from potential attacks in what century?
explanation: The Great Walls of Kano were defensive walls built to protect the city of Kano, in present-day Nigeria, from potential attacks. They were built in the 15th century.

subject: History
q: The Treaty of Nanjing, signed after the First Opium War, resulted in the cession of which territory to Great Britain?
explanation: The Treaty of Nanjing, signed in 1842, ended the First Opium War between the United Kingdom and China. As a result of the treaty, China ceded the territory of Hong Kong to the British Empire.

subject: History
q: Which of the following trade commodities was highly sought after and contributed to the rise of several states in the Nigeria area up to 1800?
explanation: Salt was a highly sought-after trade commodity that contributed to the rise of several states in the Nigeria area up to 1800. Salt was used as a medium of exchange in many parts of West Africa.

subject: History
q: Which event marked the rise of Mohammed Ali to power in Egypt?
explanation: Mohammed Ali came to power in Egypt following the Mamluk Massacre. The Mamluks were a military caste in Egypt, and their massacre by Mohammed Ali marked a significant shift in power.

subject: History
q: The Rwandan genocide in 1994 resulted in the mass killing of which ethnic group?
explanation: The Rwandan genocide was a mass slaughter of Tutsi in Rwanda during the Rwandan Civil War, which had started in 1990. It is estimated that during the approximately 100-day period from April 7 to mid-July 1994, between 500,000 and 1,000,000 Tutsis were killed, constituting an estimated 70% of the Tutsi population.

subject: History
q: What event marked the official end of apartheid in South Africa?
options: The release of Nelson Mandela from prison
The signing of the Freedom Charter
The Truth and Reconciliation Commission hearings
explanation: The official end of apartheid in South Africa was marked by the release of Nelson Mandela from prison in 1990. This was followed by the negotiations that led to the end of the apartheid regime and the establishment of a democratic government in South Africa.

subject: History
q: The Richards Constitution of 1946 introduced the concept of regionalism in Nigeria, dividing the country into how many regions?
explanation: The Richards Constitution of 1946 introduced the concept of regionalism in Nigeria, dividing the country into three regions: Northern, Western, and Eastern. This was done to decentralize power and give more autonomy to the regions, which were largely divided along ethnic lines.

subject: History
q: During The Great Trek, the Boers clashed with which indigenous African groups?
explanation: During The Great Trek, the Boers, Dutch settlers in South Africa, clashed with the Zulu and Xhosa indigenous African groups. The Great Trek was a migration of Dutch-speaking colonists inland in South Africa in the 1830s.

subject: History
q: Who was the leader of the opposition in Nigeria during the Second Republic?
explanation: During the Second Republic of Nigeria (1979-1983), the leader of the opposition was Chief Obafemi Awolowo. He was the leader of the Unity Party of Nigeria (UPN), which was one of the major opposition parties at the time.

subject: History
q: Muhammadu Buhari served as Nigeria's military head of state during which period?
explanation: Muhammadu Buhari served as Nigeria's military head of state from December 31, 1983, to August 27, 1985. This period is covered by the option '1983-1985'.

subject: History
q: The Efik Kingdom had a highly developed system of writing known as
explanation: The Efik Kingdom had a highly developed system of writing known as Nsibidi. Nsibidi is a system of symbols indigenous to what is now southeastern Nigeria that is apparently an ideographic script, though there have been suggestions that it includes logographic elements.

subject: History
q: The Oyo Empire reached its peak during the reign of which Alaafin?
explanation: The Oyo Empire reached its peak during the reign of Alaafin Abiodun. He was a ruler who expanded the empire to its greatest size and the empire enjoyed a period of peace and prosperity during his reign.

subject: History
q: Which initiative was launched by the Buhari administration to tackle corruption in Nigeria?
explanation: The Buhari administration launched the EFCC to tackle corruption in Nigeria.

subject: History
q: The Magna Carta, signed in 1215, is an important historical document that limited the power of which figure?
explanation: The Magna Carta, signed in 1215, is an important historical document that limited the power of Kings and Monarchs. It was a charter of liberties to which the English barons forced King John to give his assent. It is widely viewed as one of the most important legal documents in the development of modern democracy.

subject: History
q: What contributed significantly to the rise of early states in Nigeria?
explanation: Agricultural advancements contributed significantly to the rise of early states in Nigeria. The development of farming led to economic growth and the formation of complex societies and states. It allowed for the production of surplus food, which could support a class of people who did not need to devote their lives to farming.

subject: History
q: What was a significant change introduced by the Shehus in the administration of Borno?
explanation: The significant change introduced by the Shehus in the administration of Borno was the centralization of the military. This helped in strengthening the control and power of the Shehus over the region. The correct option that matches this answer is 'Centralization of the military'.

subject: History
q: The elective principle introduced by the Clifford Constitution allowed for elections in which Nigerian cities?
explanation: The elective principle introduced by the Clifford Constitution allowed for elections in Lagos and Calabar.

subject: History
q: The primary role of the Executive Council in colonial Nigeria was to
options: Advise the Governor-General on policy matters
Draft laws for the Legislative Council to pass
explanation: The correct answer is Advise the Governor-General on policy matters. The primary role of the Executive Council in colonial Nigeria was to advise the Governor-General on policy matters and assist in the administration of the colony.

subject: History
q: Indirect rule was most successful in
explanation: Indirect rule was most successful in Northern Nigeria. This region had a pre-existing system of administration that aligned well with the principles of indirect rule, making it easier for the British colonial authorities to govern through local traditional rulers.

subject: History
q: Nigeria's involvement in which UN agency has been crucial in addressing global health issues?
explanation:
Nigeria's involvement in the World Health Organization (WHO) has been crucial in addressing global health issues. WHC
is a specialized agency of the United Nations responsible for international public health.

subject: History
q: The Abacha regime is known for instituting which economic policy aimed at centralizing control of Nigeria's oil revenue?
options: National Economic Empowerment and Development Strategy (NEEDS)
explanation: The economic policy known for centralizing control of Nigeria's oil revenue during the Abacha regime was the 'Petroleum Trust Fund (PTF)', which aimed at managing and distributing oil revenues for developmental projects.

subject: History
q: Which Christian missionary is credited with the translation of the Bible into Yoruba?
explanation: The correct answer is Samuel Ajayi Crowther. He is credited with the translation of the Bible into Yoruba. Samuel Ajayi Crowther was a Yoruba ex-slave who became the first African Anglican bishop in Nigeria and played a significant role in the translation of the Bible into Yoruba language.

subject: History
q: One major effect of the establishment of Native Authorities was
options: The empowerment of traditional rulers within the colonial administration
The abolition of all traditional customs
The weakening of traditional power structures
The immediate modernization of local governance
explanation: The correct answer is The empowerment of traditional rulers within the colonial administration. One major effect of the establishment of Native Authorities was the empowerment of traditional rulers within the colonial administration, giving them some authority in local governance.

subject: History
q: The rise of the Kingdom of Ashanti was primarily driven by the trade of which commodity?
explanation: The rise of the Kingdom of Ashanti was primarily driven by the trade of Gold. Gold was a significant commodity in the region and played a crucial role in the economic development and power of the Ashanti Kingdom.

subject: History
q: The primary goal of early Christian missionaries in Nigeria was to
options: Spread Christianity and convert the local population
explanation: The correct answer is Spread Christianity and convert the local population. The primary goal of early Christian missionaries in Nigeria was to spread Christianity and convert the local population to the Christian faith.

subject: History
q: Benin's military campaigns often targeted which neighboring ethnic group?
explanation: Benin's military campaigns often targeted The Yoruba ethnic group. This is historically accurate as the Benin Empire engaged in military conflicts with neighboring groups, including the Yoruba.

subject: History
q: The Battle of Blood River in 1838 was fought between the Boers and which indigenous group?
explanation: The Battle of Blood River in 1838 was fought between the Boers and the Zulus.

subject: History
q: The amalgamation of 1914 resulted in the creation of
options: unified legal system for all of Nigeria
Separate administrative structures for North and South
explanation: The amalgamation of 1914 resulted in the creation of separate administrative structures for North and South. It led to the unification of the Northern and Southern Protectorates of Nigeria, creating distinct administrative regions.

subject: History
q: The Nupe kingdom reached its peak under the leadership of which notable ruler?
explanation: The Nupe Kingdom reached its peak under the leadership of Etsu (King) Mamudu. He was a significant ruler in the 19th century, known for consolidating power, expanding the kingdom, and making the Nupe a dominant force in the region. Etsu Mamudu strengthened the military and administration, contributing to the kingdom's prosperity and influence during his reign.

subject: History
q: Which policy implemented by the British in the 1930s aimed at developing infrastructure and boosting agriculture in Nigeria?
options: The Colonial Development and Welfare Act
The Ten-Year Plan of Development and Welfare
explanation: The policy implemented by the British in the 1930s aimed at developing infrastructure and boosting agriculture in Nigeria was The Colonial Development and Welfare Act. This act focused on improving the economic and social conditions in British colonies, including Nigeria.

subject: History
q: Which ancient civilization built the Great Zimbabwe?
explanation: The Great Zimbabwe was built by the Shona civilization. The correct option that matches this answer is "The Shona'. The Shona people constructed the Great Zimbabwe, which was a significant ancient city and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

subject: History
q: The rise of nationalist movements in Nigeria was significantly influenced by
options: World War II and the Atlantic Charter
explanation: The correct answer is World War II and the Atlantic Charter. The rise of nationalist movements in Nigeria was significantly influenced by the experiences of Nigerians who fought in World War II and the principles of self- determination outlined in the Atlantic Charter.

subject: History
q: Which Omani Sultan moved the capital from Muscat to Zanzibar in the 19th century?
explanation: The Omani Sultan who moved the capital from Muscat to Zanzibar in the 19th century was Seyyid Said, also known as Sa'id bin Sultan.

subject: History
q: Which of the following best describes the main cause of the Mfecane?
explanation: The correct answer is Competition over scarce resources. The main cause of the Mfecane was competition over scarce resources among various Nguni groups in southern Africa. This led to widespread warfare, displacement of populations, and social upheaval in the early 19th century.

subject: History
q: The Yoruba city-state of Ibadan rose to prominence in the 19th century primarily due to
options: Its role as a center of education
Its location on major trade routes
The military prowess of its leaders
explanation: The Yoruba city-state of Ibadan rose to prominence in the 19th century primarily due to the military prowess of its leaders. Ibadan became a dominant force in the Yoruba region through its military strength and strategic alliances, rather than its location, gold, or education.

subject: History
q: The primary economic activity that drew Europeans to Nigeria in the early period was
explanation: The primary economic activity that drew Europeans to Nigeria in the early period was the transatlantic slave trade. This trade involved the forced transportation of African people to the Americas for labor, which was a significant economic activity at that time.

subject: History
q: The capital of the Kanem-Borno Empire moved from Kanem to Borno primarily due to
explanation: The correct answer is Environmental changes. The capital of the Kanem-Borno Empire moved from Kanem to Borno primarily due to environmental changes. Borno had better access to water and was more suitable for agriculture compared to Kanem, which suffered from environmental degradation.

subject: History
q: One of the key factors that led to military intervention in Nigeria's First Republic was
options: The refusal of political leaders to implement Western education
The lack of interest in independence
explanation: The correct answer is Widespread corruption and political instability. One of the key factors that led to military intervention in Nigeria's First Republic was the widespread corruption and political instability that undermined the democratic process.

subject: History
q: Which of these leaders succeeded Usman dan Fodio as the head of the Sokoto Caliphate?
explanation:
Muhammadu Bello succeeded Usman dan Fodio as the head of the Sokoto Caliphate. He was one of the prominent leader:
of the Fulani Jihad and played a key role in the administration of the caliphate.

subject: History
q: The Pan-African Congress of 1945, which called for an end to colonial rule in Africa, was held in which city?
explanation: The Pan-African Congress of 1945, which called for an end to colonial rule in Africa, was held in Manchester. The correct option that matches this answer is 'London'. The congress was held in Manchester, England, and was attended by various African leaders and activists advocating for independence and self-governance.

subject: History
q: The Mahdiyya Movement in Sudan was primarily a response to
explanation: The Mahdiyya Movement in Sudan was primarily a response to Egyptian and Ottoman control. This movement was led by Muhammad Ahmad, who claimed to be the Mahdi, a messianic figure in Islam, and sought to overthrow the Egyptian- Ottoman rule in Sudan.

subject: History
q: Which Islamic reform leader established the Massina Empire in present-day Mali?
explanation: The Islamic reform leader who established the Massina Empire in present-day Mali was Seku Amadu (Sheikh Amadu Lobbo). He founded the empire in the early 19th century (1818) as part of a larger Islamic reform movement in West Africa aimed at establishing a theocratic state based on Islamic law (Sharia).

subject: History
q: Which of the following was a significant factor in the rise of Nigerian nationalism in the early 20th century?
options: The British colonial policy of assimilation
explanation: The rise of Nigerian nationalism in the early 20th century was significantly influenced by the introduction of indirect rule. Indirect rule system allowed for the preservation of traditional institutions and authority, which in turn led to the emergence of nationalist movements seeking self-governance within the colonial structure.

subject: History
q: What significant development in Benin's political structure occurred during the reign of Oba Ewedo?
options: The establishment of a centralized monarchy
The introduction of the use of cowries as currency
The division of the kingdom into administrative units
The establishment of trade relations with the Portuguese
explanation: The correct development was the establishment of a hereditary/centralized monarchy.

subject: History
q: Which constitution laid the foundation for federalism in Nigeria?
explanation: The Lyttleton Constitution of 1954 laid the foundation for federalism in Nigeria. This constitution established a federal system of government in Nigeria, dividing powers between the central government and regional governments, which is a key aspect of federalism.

subject: History
q: The invasion by which group significantly contributed to the fall of the Old Oyo Empire?
explanation: The invasion by the Fulani significantly contributed to the fall of the Old Oyo Empire. The Fulani Jihad led by Usman dan Fodio weakened the empire and eventually led to its collapse.

subject: History
q: Which of the following cash crops became highly significant in Nigeria during the colonial period?
explanation: The correct answer is Cocoa. Cocoa became highly significant in Nigeria during the colonial period due to its economic importance and the establishment of cocoa plantations by the colonial powers.

subject: History
q: Which Nigerian leader led the resistance against British colonization in the Aba Women's War of 1929?
explanation: Funmilayo Ransome-Kuti led the resistance against British colonization in the Aba Women's War of 1929. She was a prominent Nigerian activist and played a significant role in the women's movement in Nigeria.

subject: History
q: Which economic factor was a major influence behind the 1914 amalgamation?
options: The financial deficits in Northern Nigeria
The need to control oil resources
The desire to unify currency systems
explanation: The desire to unify currency systems was a major economic factor behind the 1914 amalgamation of Nigeria. The British colonial administration aimed to streamline economic policies and governance by unifying the currency systems in the region.

subject: History
q: Mohammad Ali Pasha is often referred to as the "founder of modern Egypt" due to his reforms in which sectors?
explanation: Mohammad Ali Pasha is often referred to as the 'founder of modern Egypt' due to his reforms in military, education, and agriculture sectors. The correct option that matches this answer is 'Military, education, and agriculture'. Mohammad Ali Pasha implemented significant reforms in these sectors, modernizing Egypt and laying the foundation for its development.

subject: History
q: The creation of the Mid-Western Region in 1963 was primarily a response to
options: The demands of the minority groups in the Niger Delta
Ethnic tensions in the Western Region
The growing influence of the NCNC
explanation: The correct answer is The demands of the minority groups in the Niger Delta. The creation of the Mid-Western Region ir 1963 was primarily a response to the demands of the minority groups in the Niger Delta who sought more autonomy an representation in the Nigerian political structure.

subject: History
q: Which of the following Yoruba states rose to prominence as a result of the decline of Oyo?
explanation: The Yoruba state that rose to prominence as a result of the decline of Oyo was Ibadan. After the decline of the Oyo Empire, Ibadan emerged as a powerful Yoruba city-state in the 19th century.

subject: History
q: Which treaty did Menelik II reject, leading to the Italian invasion and the subsequent Battle of Adwa?
explanation: The correct treaty that Menelik II rejected was the Treaty of Wuchale, which led to the Italian invasion and the Battle of Adwa.

subject: History
q: The fall of which kingdom marked a significant milestone in the British conquest of the Nigerian area?
explanation: The correct answer is Benin Kingdom in 1897. The fall of the Benin Kingdom marked a significant milestone in the British conquest of the Nigerian area as it led to the British annexation of Benin and the looting of valuable artifacts.

subject: History
q: The Sharpeville Massacre of 1960 was a turning point in the struggle against apartheid because it led to
options: The banning of the African National Congress (ANC) and Pan Africanist Congress (PAC)
International recognition of South Africa's independence
explanation:
The correct answer is Increased support for apartheid policies. The Sharpeville Massacre of 1960, where 69 people were killed by police during a peaceful protest, led to increased international condemnation of apartheid policies and
increased support for the anti-apartheid movement.

subject: History
q: Which principle was established at the Berlin Conference to justify European claims to African territories?
explanation: The principle established at the Berlin Conference to justify European claims to African territories was 'Effective occupation.' This principle required European powers to demonstrate effective control over a territory to legitimize their claims, regardless of prior African ownership or sovereignty.

subject: History
q: The Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962 brought the world to the brink of nuclear war. It was a standoff between which two superpowers?
options: The United Kingdom and the Soviet Union
The United States and the Soviet Union
explanation: The Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962 was a standoff between the United States and the Soviet Union. The crisis was a result of the placement of Soviet nuclear missiles in Cuba, which was seen as a direct threat to the United States.

subject: History
q: The humanitarian crisis during the Nigerian Civil War was exacerbated by
options: The destruction of infrastructure by the Biafran forces
A blockade imposed by the Nigerian government on Biafra
explanation: The humanitarian crisis during the Nigerian Civil War was exacerbated by a blockade imposed by the Nigerian government on Biafra. The blockade led to severe food and medical shortages in Biafra, worsening the already dire humanitarian situation during the conflict.

subject: History
q: The introduction of the 1946 Richards Constitution in Nigeria was significant because
options: It established a federal system with equal representation for all ethnic groups
It led to the abolition of traditional chieftaincies and local governance
It resulted in the complete independence of Nigeria from British control
It marked the first significant step towards self-government and regional representation
explanation: The introduction of the 1946 Richards Constitution in Nigeria was significant because it marked the first significant step towards self-government and regional representation. The constitution laid the foundation for increased Nigerian participation in governance and paved the way for future constitutional developments.

subject: History
q: Khedive Ismail's ambitious modernization projects in Egypt led to
options: Massive debt and eventual control by European powers
The unification of the Arab world under Egyptian leadership
Egypt becoming a global economic powerhouse
explanation: Khedive Ismail's ambitious modernization projects in Egypt led to massive debt and eventual control by European powers. The modernization efforts, including the construction of the Suez Canal, put Egypt in significant debt, leading to European intervention.

subject: History
q: Theodore II's efforts at unifying Ethiopia were challenged by
options: European colonizers seeking to divide the country
Religious factions within the Ethiopian Orthodox Church
Regional lords and noblemen who resisted central authority
explanation: Theodore II's efforts at unifying Ethiopia were challenged by regional lords and noblemen who resisted central authorit These local power structures posed significant obstacles to the centralization efforts of Theodore II, hindering his unification goals.

subject: History
q: The United Nations General Assembly adopted a resolution in 1973 declaring apartheid as a
explanation: The United Nations General Assembly adopted a resolution in 1973 declaring apartheid as a crime against humanity. Apartheid was a system of institutionalized racial segregation and discrimination in South Africa, and the resolution condemned it as a crime against humanity.

subject: History
q: Which of the following social changes was a direct result of urbanization in colonial Nigeria?
options: The complete assimilation of Nigerian elites into British culture
The decline of traditional chieftaincy institutions
The growth of labor unions and political activism
The eradication of ethnic and regional differences
explanation: Urbanization led to a mix of traditional and modern influences, and the growth of labor unions and political activism was one of the outcomes.

subject: History
q: Christian missionary activities in Liberia were primarily dominated by which denomination in the 19th century?
explanation: Christian missionary activities in Liberia in the 19th century were primarily dominated by the Methodist denomination The Methodist Church played a significant role in establishing missions and schools in Liberia during that period.

subject: History
q: The impact of the Mfecane on trade in Southern Africa was
options: The decline of trade networks due to increased conflict
Enhancement of trade relations with European traders
Disruption of existing trade routes and establishment of new ones
The integration of African trade systems with European markets
explanation: The impact of the Mfecane on trade in Southern Africa was the disruption of existing trade routes and the establishment of new ones. The conflicts during the Mfecane led to the reorganization of trade networks in the region.

subject: History
q: The Nupe rulers, known as the Emir, exercised control through
options: A complex system of provincial governors and military commanders
Heavy taxation and land acquisition policies
The establishment of a theocratic government
Direct control over all economic activities
explanation: The Nupe rulers, known as the Emir, exercised control through a complex system of provincial governors and military commanders. This system allowed for effective governance and control over the region.

subject: History
q: The Islamic reform movement led by Al-Hajj Umar Tall was characterized by
options: The introduction of Western education and technology in West Africa
The development of alliances with European colonial powers
The promotion of commercial agriculture and trade with Europe
The establishment of a theocratic state in Senegal and the western Sudan
explanation: The Islamic reform movement led by Al-Hajj Umar Tall was characterized by the establishment of a theocratic state in Senegal and the western Sudan. This is evident from the historical accounts of Umar Tall's efforts to establish an Islamic state based on his interpretation of Islamic law and governance.

subject: History
q: Which ECOWAS protocol allows for the free movement of persons, goods, and services within member states?
explanation: The ECOWAS protocol that allows for the free movement of persons, goods, and services within member states is The Protocol on Free Movement. This protocol aims to promote integration and economic development within the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS).

subject: History
q: The Satiru Uprising of 1906 was a significant event in colonial Nigeria. What was the primary cause of this rebellion?
options: Resistance to the imposition of colonial rule
explanation: The primary cause of the Satiru Uprising of 1906 was resistance to the imposition of colonial rule. The British colonial rule was seen as oppressive and exploitative, leading to various uprisings and rebellions across Nigeria.

subject: History
q: Mohammed Ali's economic reforms in Egypt included
options: The reduction of agricultural production and reliance on imports
The establishment of state monopolies over key industries
The introduction of a free market economy
The privatization of major state enterprises
explanation: Mohammed Ali's economic reforms in Egypt included the establishment of state monopolies over key industries. This centralized control was a key aspect of his economic policies.

subject: History
q: The Kanem-Borno Empire was one of the most powerful states in the Nigeria area during the 19th century. Its capital during this period was
explanation: During the 19th century, the capital of the Kanem-Borno Empire was Ngazargamu. This city served as the political and administrative center of the empire, showcasing its power and influence in the Nigeria area.

subject: History
q: Which of the following was a major export commodity of the Oyo Empire during the pre-colonial period?
explanation: Kola nuts were a major export commodity of the Oyo Empire during the pre-colonial period. They were highly valued fo their stimulant properties and were traded extensively within and outside the empire.

subject: History
q: The rise of the Kanem-Borno Empire in the 9th century was primarily due to
options: Diplomatic alliances with the Ottoman Empire
explanation: The empire's rise was mainly attributed to its control of trans-Saharan trade routes, which facilitated economic prosperity and political influence in the region.

subject: History
q: The infamous Middle Passage refers to
options: The journey of enslaved Africans across the Atlantic Ocean to the Americas
The migration of Africans to Europe for labor
The pathway used by missionaries in Africa
The route taken by European explorers to reach Asia
explanation:
The infamous Middle Passage refers to the journey of enslaved Africans across the Atlantic Ocean to the Americas. It wa
a horrific and inhumane voyage that resulted in immense suffering and loss of life among the enslaved population.

subject: History
q: Seku Amadu initiated a jihad in the early 19th century and established the Massina Empire. His jihad was primarily against
explanation: Seku Amadu initiated a jihad against the Sokoto Caliphate. The Sokoto Caliphate was a powerful Islamic state in the region, and Seku Amadu's jihad was aimed at challenging their authority and establishing his own empire.

subject: History
q: The activities of the British East Africa Company led to
options: The discovery of gold mines in South Africa
The establishment of protectorates in East Africa
The formation of the United Nations
The creation of a trans-Saharan trade route
explanation: The activities of the British East Africa Company led to the establishment of protectorates in East Africa. The company played a significant role in the colonization and establishment of British control in East Africa.

subject: History
q: Which economic policy introduced by the Buhari regime had a significant impact on Nigeria's foreign exchange market?
explanation: The economic policy introduced by the Buhari regime that had a significant impact on Nigeria's foreign exchange marke was the Structural Adjustment Program (SAP). SAP aimed to restructure the economy through liberalization, privatization, and deregulation, impacting various sectors including the foreign exchange market.

subject: History
q: The primary aim of the Ekumeku Movement in the Asaba hinterland was to
options: Resist British taxation and land policies
explanation: The primary aim of the Ekumeku Movement in the Asaba hinterland was to resist British taxation and land policies. This movement was a resistance movement against British colonial policies in the Asaba region.

subject: History
q: The arrival of the Portuguese on the West African coast in the 15th century marked the beginning of European influence in the region. Their primary interest was in
options: Converting the local population to Christianity
Engaging in the trans-Atlantic slave trade
explanation: The primary interest of the Portuguese upon arriving on the West African coast in the 15th century was engaging in the trans-Atlantic slave trade. This marked the beginning of a dark period in history where Africans were forcibly taken as slaves to the Americas.

subject: History
q: The Sokoto Caliphate's relations with the British colonial powers in the 19th century were
options: Generally cooperative, with the Caliphate aiding British administrative efforts
Marked by frequent conflicts and military confrontations
Based on treaties and trade agreements leading to mutual cooperation
Limited to diplomatic negotiations and sporadic trade interactions
explanation: The Sokoto Caliphate's relations with the British colonial powers in the 19th century were based on treaties and trade agreements leading to mutual cooperation. This cooperation was evident in various agreements and interactions between the Caliphate and the British authorities during that period.

subject: History
q: The rise of consular authority in Africa during the colonial era involved
options: The removal of all forms of European governance
European consuls exercising political and economic control in their respective regions
The promotion of African trade unions and cooperatives
The establishment of local African consulates to manage trade
explanation: The rise of consular authority in Africa during the colonial era involved European consuls exercising political and economic control in their respective regions. Consuls played a crucial role in representing European interests and exerting influence in African territories.

subject: History
q: The primary destination of the Voortrekkers during the Great Trek was
options: The interior regions of modern-day South Africa, such as the Transvaal and the Orange Free State
explanation: The primary destination of the Voortrekkers during the Great Trek was the interior regions of modern-day South Africa, such as the Transvaal and the Orange Free State. The Voortrekkers were Boer pastoralists who migrated inland during th Great Trek.

subject: History
q: The major policy direction of the Obasanjo administration following the death of Murtala Mohammed was
options: Preparing the country for a transition to civilian rule and democratic elections
Strengthening alliances with other military regimes in Africa
Increasing military spending and expanding Nigeria's influence in international conflicts
Pursuing aggressive land and agricultural reforms to boost the economy
explanation: The major policy direction of the Obasanjo administration following the death of Murtala Mohammed was preparing the country for a transition to civilian rule and democratic elections. Obasanjo's administration focused on the restoration c civilian rule after a period of military governance, leading to the eventual transition to democratic elections in Nigeria.

subject: History
q: The challenge of building effective political institutions in newly independent African states has been exacerbated by
options: The prevalence of democratic governance models
High levels of foreign direct investment
A lack of experienced administrative personnel and leadership
explanation: The challenge of building effective political institutions in newly independent African states has been exacerbated by a lack of experienced administrative personnel and leadership. This lack of expertise and leadership has hindered the development of stable political institutions.

subject: History
q: The concept of "The White Man's Burden," which justified European imperialism, was popularized by which British writer?
explanation: The concept of 'The White Man's Burden, which justified European imperialism, was popularized by Rudyard Kipling. Kipling's poem of the same name portrayed imperialism as a duty of the white race to civilize and uplift non-white peoples.

subject: History
q: The Omani Sultanate's control over Zanzibar led to
options: The widespread conversion of Zanzibar to Christianity
The complete cessation of the slave trade in the region
The establishment of a major center for the East African slave trade
The creation of a unified East African political entity
explanation: The Omani Sultanate's control over Zanzibar led to the establishment of a major center for the East African slave trade. Zanzibar became a significant hub for the slave trade under Omani rule.

subject: History
q: One of Gowon's major achievements was the hosting of which international event in Lagos in 1973?
options: The Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting
explanation: Gowon's major achievement was hosting The OAU Summit in Lagos in 1973. The OAU (Organization of African Unity) Summit was a significant international event that brought together African leaders to discuss and address key issues affecting the continent.

subject: History
q: One of the key impacts of French colonialism in West Africa was
options: The imposition of the French legal system and civil code
The abolition of all indigenous forms of governance
The replacement of Islam with Christianity
The promotion of Swahili as the lingua franca
explanation: One of the key impacts of French colonialism in West Africa was the imposition of the French legal system and civil code This helped in the administration and governance of the colonies by the French authorities.

subject: History
q: Which leader, influenced by the Sokoto Caliphate, established a theocratic state in Masina in the early 19th century?
explanation: The leader influenced by the Sokoto Caliphate who established a theocratic state in Masina in the early 19th century wa Seku Amadu. He was inspired by the Sokoto Caliphate's model of Islamic governance and sought to establish a similar theocratic state in Masina.

subject: History
q: The role of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC) in post-apartheid South Africa was to
options: Facilitate the redistribution of land and resources among previously disadvantaged groups
Oversee the transition of economic policies from apartheid to democracy
Enforce legal action against all members of the apartheid regime
Investigate human rights violations and promote national healing
explanation: The role of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC) in post-apartheid South Africa was to investigate human rights violations and promote national healing. The TRC played a crucial role in uncovering past atrocities, providing a platform for victims to share their stories, and fostering reconciliation in the country.

subject: History
q: The apartheid policy of "separate development" was also known as
explanation: The apartheid policy of 'separate development' was also known as Bantustans. This policy involved the creation of separate territories for different racial groups in South Africa, known as Bantustans, as part of the apartheid system.

subject: History
q: The primary impact of the Mahdist Revolt in Sudan on Ethiopia was
options: The direct military support provided to Ethiopian forces
The diversion of British and Italian resources away from Ethiopia
The establishment of a Mahdist alliance with Ethiopian rulers
The establishment of a political union between Sudan and Ethiopia
explanation: The primary impact of the Mahdist Revolt in Sudan on Ethiopia was the diversion of British and Italian resources away from Ethiopia. The Mahdist Revolt (1881-1899), led by Muhammad Ahmad al-Mahdi, occupied British and other colonia powers' attention in Sudan, reducing their focus on Ethiopia. This created a temporary window for Ethiopia to consolidate its power and resist external pressures, including colonial encroachments by European powers.

subject: History
q: The Ebira people, also from the Niger-Benue Valley, are known for their resistance to which British colonial policy in the early 20th century?
explanation: The Ebira people resisted the British colonial policy of taxation in the early 20th century. Taxation was one of the key methods used by the British to extract resources from the colonies, leading to resistance and uprisings.

subject: History
q: The British amalgamation of Nigeria in 1914 was primarily motivated by
options: desire to promote cultural unity among the diverse ethnic groups
Pressure from the League of Nations
Economic considerations, particularly the need to make the Northern Protectorate financially viable
explanation: The British amalgamation of Nigeria in 1914 was primarily motivated by economic considerations, particularly the nee to make the Northern Protectorate financially viable. By merging the Northern and Southern Protectorates, the British aimed to create a more economically sustainable administrative unit.

subject: History
q: The Voortrekker Monument, inaugurated in 1949, is a symbol of Afrikaner nationalism. It commemorates which historical event?
explanation: The Voortrekker Monument commemorates The Great Trek, which was a significant historical event in Afrikaner history The monument symbolizes the pioneering spirit and nationalism of the Afrikaner people during their migration inland i the early 19th century.

subject: History
q: The Aba Women's Riot of 1929 was triggered by:
explanation: The riot was sparked by the imposition of direct taxation on women.

subject: History

explanation: The League of Nations was established after World War I in 1919 to promote global peace but eventually failed to prevent another war.

options: divide Africa peacefully among European powers
explanation: It regulated colonisation and formalised the partition of Africa.

explanation: The Igbo political system was decentralized, with councils of elders and participatory village assemblies.

explanation:
The Nok culture, which flourished between 500 BC and 200 AD in present-day Nigeria, is notable for its early use of iron smelting,
